For John Green, the Battle Over Access to Books has Gotten Personal
nytimes.com

For John Green, the Battle Over Access to Books has Gotten Personal

NY Times · by Alexandra Alter · September 6, 2023

A fight over Green\xe2\x80\x99s books in his home state of Indiana reflects a broader cultural debate over what books are appropriate for young readers, and who gets to decide.

By

Published Aug. 31, 2023Updated Sept. 6, 2023

For about as long as he\xe2\x80\x99s been a published author, John Green has faced efforts to censor his books. His debut novel, \xe2\x80\x9cLooking for Alaska,\xe2\x80\x9d a coming-of-age story that includes references to drug use and sex, has been challenged in schools for at least 15 years, and has frequently landed on the American Library Association\xe2\x80\x99s most banned books list. Last year, it received more than 50 challenges in schools across the country.

But a recent dust-up over whether his books are appropriate for teens feels more personal, and like an escalation of a growing movement to ban and restrict access to books, Green said.

A public library in his home state of Indiana implemented a new policy earlier this year requiring library staff to remove any books with sexually explicit content from the children\xe2\x80\x99s and teens section and re-shelve them in the adult collection. The decision at Hamilton East Public Library in Noblesville meant more than 1,800 young adult books were moved, among them classics like \xe2\x80\x9cForever\xe2\x80\x9d by Judy Blume and \xe2\x80\x9cSpeak\xe2\x80\x9d by Laurie Halse Anderson, as well as two of Green\xe2\x80\x99s novels, \xe2\x80\x9cLooking for Alaska\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9cThe Fault in Our Stars.\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cI love Indiana so much, and it breaks my heart to see that kind of radicalism in a public library,\xe2\x80\x9d said Green, who lives in Indianapolis.

The mass relocation of Y.A. titles at Hamilton East has drawn intense public scrutiny in part because Green\xe2\x80\x99s beloved books were swept up in the purge. But it\xe2\x80\x99s hardly an isolated incident.

es in Indianapolis. The mass relocation of Y.A. titles at Hamilton East has drawn intense public scrutiny in part because Green\xe2\x80\x99s beloved books were swept up in the purge. But it\xe2\x80\x99s hardly an isolated incident. Thank you for your patience while we verify access. Already a subscriber? Log in . Want all of The Times? Subscribe .
